questions.question: In Chord, what does successor(k) return?
options: The node that hashed to k
The first actual node at or after identifier k, going clockwise around the ring
The node immediately before k on the ring
The node that originally stored the key k
questions.explanation: successor(k) is the first live node at or following k clockwise. With nodes {1,4,7,12,15,20,27}, successor(6)=7 and successor(8)=12. This is the function that assigns each key to the node responsible for storing it.

questions.question: What does finger-table entry i of a node with identifier k contain, and why does this give O(log n) lookups?
options: The IP of node k+i; each hop moves one step around the ring
start = (k + 2^i) mod 2^m and successor(start); each successive entry doubles the distance, so each hop at least halves the remaining distance to the target
A random node identifier; randomness ensures load balancing
The predecessor of k; lookups walk backward to the key
questions.explanation: Entry i points to successor(k + 2^i). The starts double each step, so the finger table gives a node exponentially spaced long-distance pointers. A lookup forwards to the closest preceding finger, which halves the remaining distance, giving O(log n) average hops.

questions.question: Compare linear successor-walk lookup with finger-table lookup for a ring of n nodes. Which statement is correct?
options: Both take O(log n) hops on average
Linear walk takes O(n) and finger lookup takes O(log n), so finger lookup is exponentially faster for large n
Linear walk takes O(1) and finger lookup takes O(n)
Both take O(n) hops but finger lookup uses less bandwidth
questions.explanation: A linear walk follows the immediate successor chain and averages n/2 hops. The finger table routes through exponentially spaced pointers, so the average number of hops is log n. For a million nodes this is the difference between 500,000 and 20 hops.

questions.question: Why does Chord hash both node IP addresses and content names into the same identifier space?
options: So that nodes and keys can be compared for equality
So that a key can be mapped to a responsible node by the same successor() function that maps node identifiers, distributing keys uniformly at random over the nodes
To encrypt the content so only the responsible node can read it
To reduce the number of bits needed for addresses
questions.explanation: By hashing both nodes and keys with the same function into the same m-bit ring, successor(key) deterministically identifies the node responsible for a key. The uniformity of the hash spreads keys evenly across nodes, and no central assignment is needed.
